text:
OUTPATIENT ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Hematology/Oncology -- Gastrointestinal Oncology Service

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Physician]
FELLOW: [Fellow Physician]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Okafor is a 59-year-old Nigerian-American male with metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, pT3N2aM1b with bilobar hepatic and bilateral pulmonary metastases) presenting today for routine follow-up after restaging CT imaging demonstrating stable disease on ongoing second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab therapy. He also follows up regarding ongoing peripheral neuropathy, blood pressure management, and clinical trial pre-screening.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Okafor was initially diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 17-18 months ago when he presented with iron-deficiency anemia and was found on colonoscopy to have a 4.8 cm sigmoid colon mass. Staging workup at diagnosis revealed synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases (four lesions, largest 3.2 cm in segment VII). Pathology confirmed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma (grade 2). Molecular profiling showed KRAS G12D mutation, MSS/MMR-intact status, BRAF V600E wild-type, NRAS wild-type, and HER2 non-amplified, rendering him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy and checkpoint inhibition. Repeat NGS at ~14 months identified acquired HER2 amplification as a potential clonal evolution/resistance mechanism, along with persistent KRAS G12D, TP53 R248W, APC R1450*, SMAD4 R361H, PIK3CA E545K, and PTEN loss. He also has underlying SMAD4 loss, which was noted from the outset as a poor prognostic marker.

First-line treatment consisted of FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. After 8 cycles, oxaliplatin was discontinued due to cumulative dose-limiting grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (occurring in the setting of underlying mild diabetic peripheral neuropathy at baseline), and he was maintained on 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab. He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy at approximately 7 months for the primary tumor (with the liver metastases left in situ given bilobar disease and continued systemic response). He completed approximately 12 total cycles of FOLFOX-based therapy followed by maintenance, then progressed on maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab at 10 months with new bilateral pulmonary nodules (confirmed FDG-avid on PET/CT, consistent with metastatic disease) and mild hepatic progression, advancing his stage to M1b.

He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab approximately 6-7 months ago. UGT1A1 genotyping revealed *1/*28 heterozygosity (intermediate metabolizer). Cycle 2 was complicated by grade 3 diarrhea requiring IV hydration; irinotecan was subsequently dose-reduced to 150 mg/m2 (from 180 mg/m2) and has been continued at this dose without recurrence of severe diarrhea. Ramucirumab required intensification of antihypertensive management. He achieved a partial response at the cycle 4 restaging assessment (CEA down to 41.2 ng/mL from 62.3 at progression; hepatic target lesions decreased ~24% from their nadir at progression).

The most recent restaging CT performed approximately one week ago showed stable disease: hepatic lesions essentially unchanged (segment II 1.1 cm, segment IVa 1.2 cm, segment VI 0.8 cm, segment VII 1.9 cm), pulmonary nodules stable to minimally changed (right lower lobe 7 mm, left upper lobe 6 mm, right middle lobe 5 mm). No new lesions were identified. No peritoneal or ascitic disease. CEA is now 49.8 ng/mL, mildly up from the nadir of 41.2 but below the pre-treatment level and not at a level that independently triggers a change in management. CA 19-9 is 118 U/mL.

Today, Mr. Okafor comes in to discuss the imaging results in detail, review ongoing tolerability, check in on the clinical trial pre-screening process, and address quality-of-life issues including fatigue, neuropathy, and his ability to maintain his professional and personal activities.

He describes his current overall status as "managing, but tired." He continues to work part-time in an administrative capacity at his school district (he has stepped back from his role as principal to a district administrative consultant role). He reports:

- Fatigue: Persistent, grade 1, worse in the afternoon. He rates energy at 6/10 on a typical day. He notes he can complete a half-day of office work but does not have the stamina for a full in-person school day. He paces himself.
- Neuropathy: Ongoing bilateral distal sensory neuropathy, grade 2. Feet are more affected than hands. He describes numbness, tingling, and a sensation of "walking on sand" in both feet. He has noticed some improvement in his hands compared to the peak of his neuropathy -- he is now able to button shirts with concentration though still finds it challenging. Balance is occasionally impaired; he uses a walking cane when on uneven terrain or for longer walks. He has been seen by neurology and has an upcoming follow-up. Gabapentin 300 mg TID continues to provide partial symptomatic benefit; he describes tingling as attenuated but not resolved.
- GI symptoms: Diarrhea is well-controlled since irinotecan dose reduction. He has 2-3 loose stools per day (baseline for him on this regimen), no episodes of frank watery diarrhea or urgency requiring loperamide for the past several cycles. Appetite is fair; he has lost approximately 6 lbs since diagnosis (current weight 82 kg, BMI ~27.2), but weight has been stable over the past 3 months.
- Alopecia: Grade 1-2. He shaved his head early in treatment and has adapted. His wife and family have been supportive.
- Blood pressure: He checks BP daily at home. Readings generally 130-145/82-90 mmHg. He takes all three antihypertensives (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg) consistently.
- Diabetes: Continues metformin 1000 mg BID. He has been monitoring blood sugars. Fasting glucose typically in the 110-130 range. HbA1c last checked 6 months ago at 7.1%; due for recheck.
- Dyspnea: The mild exertional dyspnea he noted at progression has improved. He walks approximately 20 minutes on most days without significant breathlessness. He attributes prior dyspnea largely to anxiety about his disease status.
- No fever, chills, or night sweats. No abdominal pain or bloating. No rectal bleeding (post-colectomy). No headaches or visual changes. No bone pain.
- Psychosocially: He reports that the recent imaging showing stability has been reassuring. His wife accompanies him today. He expresses some anxiety about the CEA trending upward and asks directly about what this means for his disease trajectory. He is emotionally engaged and well-informed.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1, stable weight (82 kg, stable x 3 months). No fevers.
- HEENT: No vision changes, no mucositis.
- Cardiovascular: Hypertension managed on triple therapy. No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
- Pulmonary: Known bilateral pulmonary metastases, currently stable. Mild exertional dyspnea improved.
- GI: 2-3 loose stools/day (baseline on regimen), no grade 3 diarrhea. No nausea/vomiting. No abdominal pain. No melena or hematochezia.
- GU: No hematuria, no dysuria.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain or joint pain.
- Neurological: Bilateral distal sensory peripheral neuropathy, grade 2 (more prominent feet than hands, improved from prior grade 3). Balance occasionally impaired.
- Dermatologic: Grade 1-2 alopecia. No hand-foot syndrome, rash, or mucous membrane changes.
- Endocrine: Type 2 diabetes, well-controlled.
- Psychiatric: Mild situational anxiety regarding disease status; no depression per PHQ-2 screen (score 1).

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS), Stage IV (pT3N2aM1b) -- as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus (pre-existing, well-controlled on metformin)
3. Hypertension (pre-existing, now exacerbated by bevacizumab and ramucirumab; currently on triple therapy)
4. Hyperlipidemia
5. Laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis (~7 months after diagnosis)
6. Port-a-cath placement (right chest wall)
7. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (grade 3 at nadir; now grade 2 stable, on gabapentin)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Mr. Okafor is a Nigerian-American male, currently age 59. Former smoker (quit ~13 years ago; 15 pack-year history). Social alcohol use (reports no drinking since diagnosis). Married with two adult children. Transitioned from high school principal to district administrative consultant role during treatment. College-educated; highly engaged with his medical care and research-oriented. Lives at home with his wife. Strong social support system. Does not use recreational drugs.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of colorectal cancer or other GI malignancies. Father had prostate cancer (treated successfully). Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes. No known Lynch syndrome or other hereditary cancer syndromes (germline panel at diagnosis was negative).

ALLERGIES:
NKDA. No prior infusion reactions to any chemotherapy agents administered to date.

MEDICATIONS:
1. Irinotecan 150 mg/m2 IV day 1 every 14 days (dose-reduced from 180 mg/m2)
2. Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1 every 14 days
3. 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1 every 14 days
4. 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours every 14 days
5. Ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV day 1 every 14 days
6. Gabapentin 300 mg PO TID (for neuropathic symptoms)
7. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID (diabetes)
8. Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily (hypertension/anti-VEGF effect)
9. Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily (hypertension/anti-VEGFR2 effect)
10. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg PO daily (added for blood pressure optimization)
11. Ondansetron 8 mg PO PRN nausea
12. Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO PRN breakthrough nausea
13. Loperamide 2 mg PO PRN (diarrhea; rarely needed currently)
14. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO at bedtime (hyperlipidemia)
15. Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (cardiovascular risk)
16. Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GI prophylaxis)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: BP 136/84 mmHg (left arm, sitting), HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 16/min, Temp 98.2 F, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 82.0 kg (stable), Height 178 cm, BMI 25.9 kg/m2 (note: BMI slightly lower than prior due to weight loss, though stable recently), ECOG PS 1.

General: Well-appearing Nigerian-American male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x4. Sitting comfortably, dressed in business casual attire. No pallor or jaundice visible. Appears stated age.

HEENT: Head normocephalic. Scalp shows sparse, diffuse fine hair regrowth (consistent with mild alopecia post-irinotecan). Sclerae anicteric. Conjunctivae without pallor. Oropharynx moist without mucositis or ulceration. No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy.

Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Good air entry throughout. Chest wall: port-a-cath site right chest wall -- clean, non-tender, without erythema, induration, or signs of infection. Port accessed without difficulty.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No palpable hepatomegaly (liver edge palpated at costal margin on deep inspiration, non-tender). No splenomegaly. Healed laparoscopic colectomy scars are well-healed and non-tender. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds normoactive throughout.

Extremities: No clubbing. Mild bilateral ankle edema (trace, non-pitting). Peripheral pulses intact. No calf tenderness.

Neurological: Alert, oriented, appropriate. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities. Sensory examination: diminished light touch and vibration sense in bilateral feet (from toes to mid-foot; improved from prior examination when deficits extended to the ankle). Diminished light touch in fingertips bilaterally (mild). Deep tendon reflexes: Achilles reflex absent bilaterally (consistent with peripheral neuropathy). Patellar reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Upper extremity reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Gait: slightly wide-based but stable on flat surfaces; balance testing shows mild impairment on tandem gait.

Skin: No rash, no hand-foot syndrome. No telangiectasias or petechiae.

---

LAB RESULTS (today's visit / most recent available):
- CEA: 49.8 ng/mL (prior: 41.2 ng/mL at cycle 4 restaging; nadir ~35.2 ng/mL on maintenance; pre-treatment 62.3 ng/mL at second-line initiation; at diagnosis 187.3 ng/mL)
- CA 19-9: 118 U/mL (prior: 102 U/mL at cycle 4 restaging; at diagnosis 412 U/mL)
- CBC: WBC 4.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable), Plt 198 x10^9/L
- Absolute neutrophil count: 2.9 x10^9/L (adequate, no neutropenia)
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 138 mEq/L, K 3.9 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO2 25 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR >60), glucose 122 mg/dL (fasting)
- LFTs: AST 32 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, Alk Phos 84 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, Albumin 3.8 g/dL (all within normal limits; no hepatic dysfunction despite ongoing liver metastases)
- LDH: 198 U/L (mildly elevated upper limit of normal at our institution is 190 U/L)
- HbA1c: 7.3% (due for recheck today; mildly above target of <7% but acceptable in the oncology context given steroid use and disease burden)
- Urine protein/creatinine ratio: 0.28 (mildly elevated; monitoring for ramucirumab-related proteinuria; below threshold for dose modification per protocol; will recheck next cycle)
- Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, within normal limits
- Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (lower limit of normal; will supplement with magnesium oxide 400 mg BID)

---

IMAGING RESULTS (most recent CT scan reviewed at today's visit):
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast, performed approximately one week ago, reviewed in detail with patient at today's visit.

Liver: The four known hepatic metastases remain present but are all essentially the same size as the prior scan from approximately 8 weeks ago. The dominant segment VII lesion measures about 1.9 cm -- it has come down considerably from its peak of 3.2 cm at diagnosis. The segment IVa lesion is 1.2 cm (was 2.1 cm at diagnosis). The segment VI lesion is 0.8 cm (was 1.7 cm at diagnosis). The small segment II lesion is 1.1 cm (was 1.4 cm at diagnosis). None of these have grown appreciably, and the segment VI lesion may actually be slightly smaller than before. No new hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilation. No ascites.

Lungs: The three bilateral pulmonary nodules that first appeared at 10 months are stable to very minimally changed. The right lower lobe nodule remains 7 mm, the right middle lobe nodule is 5 mm (unchanged), and the left upper lobe nodule is 6 mm (was 5 mm at prior scan, so essentially stable given measurement variability). These nodules are not growing in any alarming fashion.

Anastomotic site: No evidence of local recurrence at the prior sigmoid colectomy anastomosis.

No peritoneal implants, no lymphadenopathy above threshold for concern, no bone lesions, no adrenal masses.

Overall: The disease picture on this scan is one of stability -- none of the lesions are meaningfully larger, and the disease burden remains significantly lower than at the time of diagnosis. The CEA mild uptick (from 41.2 to 49.8 ng/mL) is being monitored but does not by itself indicate clinical progression, particularly given stable imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Okafor is a 59-year-old male with KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV (M1b) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with bilobar hepatic and bilateral pulmonary metastases. He is approximately 17-18 months from initial diagnosis, currently on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, and showing stable disease on today's restaging review. Molecular profiling at ~14 months identified acquired HER2 amplification as a new finding, which we are tracking as a potential therapeutic target for future lines.

1. METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER -- STABLE DISEASE ON SECOND-LINE FOLFIRI PLUS RAMUCIRUMAB:
The imaging is reassuring and demonstrates that the disease is under control, albeit not shrinking further from the peak response achieved early in the FOLFIRI/ramucirumab course. I discussed the CEA trend at length with Mr. Okafor and his wife. I explained that a CEA of 49.8 -- while somewhat above the nadir of ~35 -- remains far below where we started at second-line initiation (62.3) and dramatically below diagnosis (187.3). The imaging correlates well with what we expected: stable lesions, no new disease. I reassured them that we do not make treatment decisions on CEA alone when imaging is stable and there are no new clinical symptoms of concern.

Plan: Continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at current doses (irinotecan 150 mg/m2, ramucirumab 8 mg/kg). Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 8 weeks. I will continue to follow CEA and CA 19-9 with each cycle. If at any point the imaging demonstrates unequivocal progression (either meaningful growth of known lesions or new sites of disease), we will reassess the treatment strategy at that time.

We discussed the general trajectory: FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab has data supporting a median progression-free survival of approximately 5.7 months from the RAISE trial, and he has now been on this regimen for 6-7 months -- an encouraging sign. The plan is to continue it as long as it is controlling the disease and the toxicity profile remains acceptable.

2. ACQUIRED HER2 AMPLIFICATION -- CLINICAL TRIAL PLANNING FOR FUTURE LINES:
The repeat biopsy and liquid biopsy at 14 months confirmed high-level HER2 amplification (copy number 8 on tissue, 6 on ctDNA), in the setting of KRAS G12D persistence. This HER2 amplification may represent clonal selection under prior anti-VEGF pressure or a co-existing subclone that has expanded. In the colorectal cancer setting, HER2 amplification (seen in approximately 2-3% of CRC at baseline, higher in later lines) is an emerging actionable target. Trastuzumab plus pertuzumab and trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd, Enhertu) have shown activity in HER2-amplified/overexpressing metastatic CRC in clinical trials. The MOUNTAINEER and DESTINY-CRC01/02 trials are informative in this space. We will consider these as options at next progression.

Regarding KRAS G12D: investigational KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors are in early clinical development in CRC. I have referred Mr. Okafor to our early phase trials team. Pre-screening results are pending; the trials team will contact the family if a suitable study opens and he meets eligibility criteria. The interplay between HER2 amplification and KRAS G12D mutation may complicate single-agent KRAS inhibitor activity (as KRAS-mutant tumors with co-activating pathway alterations like PIK3CA E545K + PTEN loss may have intrinsic resistance mechanisms), and this will be weighed when considering enrollment.

3. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY -- GRADE 2, STABLE:
Neuropathy improved from its nadir of grade 3 following oxaliplatin discontinuation approximately 9 months ago. Currently stable at grade 2. Not worsening on irinotecan (as expected -- irinotecan does not contribute to peripheral neuropathy). Gabapentin 300 mg TID being continued; I discussed whether to uptitrate to 600 mg TID to improve symptom control but patient reports the current dose is tolerable and he is concerned about drowsiness. Will hold at current dose. Neurology follow-up appointment in 6 weeks -- will defer to their judgment regarding further pharmacologic management. Occupational therapy has been helpful; patient is using assistive adaptive devices and has modified his home environment (non-slip mats, grab bars in shower). I encouraged him to continue physical therapy exercises for balance. I emphasized that nerve recovery after cumulative oxaliplatin toxicity can continue slowly for 12-18 months but may not fully resolve; realistic expectations discussed.

4. HYPERTENSION -- CONTROLLED ON TRIPLE ANTIHYPERTENSIVE THERAPY:
BP today 136/84 mmHg on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg + HCTZ 12.5 mg. This is well-controlled and near goal. Ramucirumab-associated hypertension has been a manageable challenge. No changes to antihypertensives today. Will continue current regimen. Urine protein/creatinine ratio mildly elevated at 0.28 -- below the threshold for ramucirumab dose modification (most guidelines use >2 g/24h as the threshold for hold) but above the lower normal range. Will recheck with next cycle labs. I explained to the patient that the ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) serves a dual purpose: blood pressure control and some renoprotection given the mild proteinuria in the setting of anti-VEGFR therapy.

5. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS:
HbA1c today 7.3%, slightly above our target of <7% for this patient. Glucose 122 mg/dL fasting. This mild elevation likely multifactorial: intermittent dexamethasone use as antiemetic during infusions, reduced physical activity due to fatigue and neuropathy, and underlying disease. Continuing metformin 1000 mg BID. Will not add additional antidiabetic agents at this time given the relatively modest elevation and complexity of his overall regimen. Recommended dietary adjustments (reduce refined carbohydrates, increase fiber). Recheck HbA1c in 3 months. Will defer more intensive diabetic management to his primary care physician.

6. ANEMIA:
Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 11.4 g/dL) -- stable compared to prior values. This is consistent with anemia of chronic disease in the setting of metastatic cancer and ongoing chemotherapy. Iron studies were checked last cycle: ferritin was 148 ng/mL (not iron deficient). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not indicated per current oncology guidelines in this context. Will monitor; transfusion threshold would be Hgb <8 g/dL or symptomatic anemia, neither of which applies currently.

7. MAGNESIUM:
Mild hypomagnesemia noted today (1.8 mEq/L). Ramucirumab can contribute to electrolyte disturbances. Starting magnesium oxide 400 mg BID. Dietary counseling given (magnesium-rich foods: leafy greens, nuts, legumes -- the latter of which he tolerates well post-colectomy). Recheck next cycle.

8. QUALITY OF LIFE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
I spent significant time today discussing Mr. Okafor's quality of life and his goals. He is remarkably resilient -- continuing to contribute professionally in a modified capacity, maintaining his home life with his wife, and staying engaged with his care. He expressed some grief about his reduced professional role but said that the work is still meaningful to him. His wife is a significant source of strength and is emotionally well-supported herself per her report. Social work has been engaged previously; I encouraged a refresher visit given the emotional weight of now being in second-line therapy. He is well-informed and directly asked about prognosis; I framed this in terms of continued disease control being our goal, acknowledging that this disease remains metastatic but that he has defied some of the initial predictions and we have further options including clinical trials and HER2-directed approaches should the current regimen stop working.

9. RETURN VISITS AND NEXT STEPS:
- Continue FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab on current schedule (every 14 days)
- Labs with next infusion (CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9, urine protein/creatinine)
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 8 weeks
- Neurology follow-up in 6 weeks for neuropathy reassessment
- HbA1c recheck in 3 months (coordinate with primary care)
- Magnesium recheck with next cycle labs
- Early phase clinical trials team to contact patient regarding KRAS G12D inhibitor trial eligibility
- Social work follow-up encouraged
- Patient instructed to call the triage nurse line for: diarrhea >4 stools above baseline per day, fever >100.4°F, severe nausea/vomiting, significant change in blood pressure (>160/100 persistently at home), worsening dyspnea, chest pain, or new neurologic symptoms

Time spent: 55 minutes (greater than half of encounter time spent in counseling and coordination of care).

Patient and wife verbalized understanding of the plan. Questions answered. Follow-up scheduled.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
[Reviewed and co-signed by Fellow]
